text.title: Auto Rollback Policy Design Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the auto-rollback-policy-design methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Policy MUST define per-axis rollback thresholds: outcome drop > 5pp triggers rollback; trajectory drop > 10pp triggers rollback; resources cost increase > 50% triggers rollback. Any axis triggering causes a full rollback.
rule.rationale: Thresholds are calibrated against eval noise floor (typical ±2pp); below noise floor is too tight, above 10pp is too loose.

rule.statement: Threshold check MUST use a rolling window of ≥ 100 inferences OR 5 minutes, whichever is longer. Single-inference triggers are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Per-inference triggers cause flapping; rolling window absorbs noise while still catching real regressions.

rule.statement: Rollout MUST start at ≤ 5% traffic; auto-rollback applies during canary. Baseline arm receives 95% traffic and provides the comparator. Promotion to 100% requires 6h clean canary at 5% + 6h clean at 25% + manual approve.

rationale.source: Google ML deployment SRE handbook; LinkedIn model-rollback notes

rule.rationale: Canary ratio caps blast radius; 5% / 25% steps match standard SRE practice without over-engineering.

rule.statement: On-call MUST have a manual-rollback override that fires immediately without threshold gating. Override usage MUST be logged with reason + reviewer in the postmortem queue.
rule.rationale: Threshold systems miss novel failure modes; manual override is the safety net but its use must be visible to prevent silent override-creep.

rule.statement: Every auto-rollback MUST trigger a 4-step runbook: capture-state (logs + traces + canary slice) → notify owner → freeze deploys → schedule postmortem within 5 working days.
rule.rationale: Without runbook, rolled-back changes get re-deployed naively. Postmortem is the only way to learn from each event.
